responses.question: What situations or places do you avoid because of anxiety?
responses.answer: Umm nothing. I am comfortable in social settings, public transport, new places. Anxiety is not a barrier for me.

responses.question: Describe how anxiety affects your decision-making or confidence.
responses.answer: Hmm confidence is good. I am known as someone who takes initiative at work. Decisions I make clearly without much hesitation.

responses.question: Describe how long your anxious thoughts usually last and how you cope with them.
responses.answer: Umm concerns pass very quickly. A short walk or good sleep is enough. I don't feel anxiety as a significant problem.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: I keep getting thoughts that I have not locked the door or turned off the gas, even when I have checked multiple times. Sometimes I also get very disturbing thoughts that I might harm my baby sister without wanting to. These thoughts horrify me but I cannot make them stop no matter how hard I try.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: I check the front door lock at least 10 to 15 times every morning before leaving for office. I also check gas knobs, switches, and windows. I know I have checked but somehow my mind does not feel satisfied and I have to go back and check again and again.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: If I cannot complete my checking routine I feel extreme dread the entire day. Once I reached office and had such strong panic about the gas that I took auto-rickshaw back home just to check, even though I was sure I had turned it off. My boss was very angry.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: I am always late to office because of checking rituals. I have received two written warnings this year. My commute which should be 30 minutes often becomes 60 minutes because I go back home to re-check. My family is also frustrated with my constant checking.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Almost no control over either. The thoughts come without invitation and the checking feels completely compulsive. I hate doing it and I hate that I cannot stop. It is like someone else is controlling my actions and I am just watching helplessly.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: I am constantly thinking that I am contaminated with germs and dirt, especially after touching anything outside my home. I get thoughts that I will make my whole family sick if I don't clean myself properly. Even after washing my hands I feel unclean and the thoughts keep returning.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: I wash my hands minimum 20 to 30 times a day. After coming from outside I take a full bath before touching anything in the house including my children. My hands have become very dry and cracked from excessive washing. I use three soaps a week.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: If I cannot wash properly — say there is no water or I am in someone else's house — I feel so contaminated and anxious that I cannot touch food or my children. I stood outside my own kitchen for one hour once because I could not wash properly.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: I avoid taking children to parks, relatives' houses, markets because contamination fear becomes very overwhelming. Husband has started managing most outdoor tasks. I spend hours every day in washing and cleaning rituals. My skin doctor says I need to stop but I cannot.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Very little control at all. I have tried many times to reduce washing but the anxiety becomes so unbearable that I always give in. My family prays for me and says I should go to temple, but I feel too contaminated to even enter temple properly.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: I keep having thoughts about numbers and symmetry. If I step on a tile with my right foot I must also step on a matching tile with left foot or something terrible will happen to my family. I know this is irrational but I cannot convince myself of that when the thought comes.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: I arrange all objects on my desk in perfect symmetry every morning. If I write with pen and it feels wrong I tear the paper and rewrite. I reread paragraphs multiple times until they feel perfectly right even when there is no practical reason to reread them.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: If symmetry or order is disturbed and I cannot fix it I feel very intense anxiety and a sense of impending disaster. I once missed a wedding ceremony because I kept rearranging items in the car. My wife was very upset. I could not explain why I was unable to stop.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: Simple tasks take very long time for me. Writing one report can take three times longer because I keep redoing things until they feel perfectly right. I have missed important meetings. My colleagues think I am slow but they do not understand what is actually happening.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: No meaningful control. I have tried stopping the rituals but the anxiety is so intense and so physical that I always eventually give in and complete them. The temporary relief makes me do it again next time. It is a trap I cannot escape from.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: I am a religious person but I keep getting thoughts that are blasphemous or disrespectful of God, which disturbs me very deeply. The more I try to push these thoughts away the stronger they become. I feel like I am a terrible sinner even though I do not believe these thoughts represent my real feelings.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: I do puja two to three times a day to neutralise the bad thoughts. I recite specific mantras a fixed number of times — must be in exact multiples of 108. If I lose count I must start over completely. I visit temple daily and have spent large amounts on donations trying to undo what I feel are sins of thought.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: If I cannot complete my puja or if I lose mantra count I feel that something terrible will befall my family as punishment. I once fasted for three days as self-punishment when I could not complete rituals properly during a work trip. My family was very worried.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: I spend four to five hours daily on religious rituals which are driven by anxiety rather than genuine devotion. My family thinks I am very religious but actually I am suffering deeply. Work is affected. My wife says our quality time together has completely disappeared.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: The thoughts are completely involuntary and deeply distressing. The rituals give some temporary relief but the thoughts always return and the rituals become longer and longer over time. A pandit told me I need psychiatric help but I felt ashamed and have not gone.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: I am in second year engineering and I keep getting intrusive thoughts that I may have cheated in exams even though I know I have not. I also get thoughts that I may have accidentally hurt someone while riding my bicycle, and I feel compelled to go back and check the road even when I know it is completely irrational.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: I mentally recount everything I did during an exam to confirm I didn't cheat, which takes hours of my time. When riding bicycle I stop and turn back two or three times to check that no one fell. I seek reassurance from friends constantly that I am a good person, which gives only brief relief.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: If I cannot complete my mental checking or get the reassurance I need I feel tremendous guilt and anxiety that doesn't go away. I once missed an important lab session because I was stuck in a reassurance-seeking loop with my roommate for two hours about whether I was a good person.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: My academic performance has dropped significantly from first year. I spend enormous mental energy on obsessive thoughts instead of studies. My CGPA has fallen nearly a full point. Friendships are strained because I ask for reassurance so repeatedly that it becomes exhausting for others.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Very little control. I know the thoughts are irrational — my mind accepts this intellectually but my emotions cannot. The rituals and reassurance-seeking provide temporary relief that quickly evaporates. I am exhausted by this constant battle in my own head.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: I am a new mother and I keep having horrifying intrusive thoughts that I might accidentally drop my baby or that something terrible will happen to her. I know I would never hurt her but the thoughts are very vivid and extremely distressing. They come constantly throughout the day.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: I check on my baby every 10 minutes even when she is sleeping peacefully. I have installed four cameras around the cot. I don't allow anyone else to hold her including my own mother because I fear they might drop her. I read about SIDS and baby accidents for hours every day.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: If I cannot check on her or if the camera connection drops even briefly I feel intense panic and have to physically go check. I have not slept more than two continuous hours since she was born three months ago. Husband says I am becoming unwell but I cannot stop.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: I cannot rest even when baby is sleeping. I cannot leave the house because monitoring is impossible outside. My physical health has deteriorated severely from sleep deprivation. I have no time for husband, hygiene, eating properly. I am running on empty.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: No control over thoughts. I understand intellectually that my baby is healthy and safe but the thoughts ignore this. The checking reduces anxiety for a few minutes then it surges back. My gynaecologist has referred me to psychiatrist but I feel ashamed to go.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: I keep having thoughts that I may have said something offensive or hurtful to someone during conversations, even completely casual ones. After every interaction I go over everything I said trying to find something wrong. I also worry I may have made accounting errors at work even after checking thoroughly.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: After any conversation I mentally replay every word I said for hours. I review my accounting work four to five times before submitting even for simple entries. I send follow-up apology messages to people after normal conversations in case I said something offensive.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: If I cannot review a conversation or check my work thoroughly I feel a persistent sense of having done something terrible wrong. I once spent an entire weekend mentally replaying a casual lunch conversation with a colleague and sent her three apology messages that left her very confused.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: I work very slowly because of all the re-checking. In a CA firm being slow is a serious problem. My principal has spoken to me twice. I avoid social gatherings because the post-event mental reviewing exhausts me completely for days after.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Minimal control. I know rationally that I have not done anything wrong but emotionally the doubt is completely overwhelming. The reviewing gives brief relief before another doubt emerges. I have been living with this since college and it keeps getting progressively worse.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: I have thoughts that I may be gay even though I am attracted to women and happily married. The thought itself doesn't define me but I cannot stop ruminating over it for hours and testing myself with mental experiments to prove it isn't true. I know this is irrational but it consumes enormous mental energy.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: I constantly test my attractions by forcing myself to look at images to confirm my orientation. I seek reassurance from wife indirectly through questions. I avoid watching any media with male bonding or friendship because it triggers my rumination for hours afterward.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: If I cannot complete a mental testing routine I feel extreme uncertainty and anxiety for the entire day. This seems completely absurd to me intellectually but the feeling is very powerful. I have ruined several social outings by being mentally absent due to this rumination.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: I lose many productive hours every day to this obsession. It has affected my intimacy with wife and she thinks I am unhappy with her, which adds another layer of distress. I cannot focus at work. I have lost significant weight from the constant stress.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: No meaningful control at all. I desperately want to stop but the thoughts keep returning. I found out this is actually a recognised OCD subtype called HOCD from an online forum. Knowing this helped me feel less alone but I have not yet sought professional help due to the stigma involved.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: Sometimes thoughts about pending work tasks enter my mind during leisure time. Normal things like remembering a call I need to make or something I need to buy. These are manageable and proportionate to my actual life situation, not distressing or intrusive.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: I maintain a to-do list and check items off as I complete them. This is a practical organizational habit that helps me feel productive, not an anxiety-driven compulsion. I do this once in the morning. It makes me feel organized and in control in a healthy way.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: If I miss my exercise routine one day I feel mildly disappointed but adapt without any significant distress. I am flexible with my habits and routines. Missing something occasionally does not cause me anxiety or a sense of impending disaster.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: My daily functioning is consistently good and healthy. I am productive at work and present at home. I don't spend excessive time on any repetitive behaviors. There is no meaningful interference in my work, relationships, or responsibilities.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: I feel good control over my thoughts and everyday behaviors. When a thought comes I can dismiss it or act on it appropriately. I don't feel driven by unwanted thoughts or compulsive urges. I feel generally in control of my mental life.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: During stressful periods like annual exams I think about studies more frequently. But these thoughts are purposeful and connected to real goals. They motivate preparation rather than cause distress. Between stressful periods my mind is generally calm and relaxed.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: Before exams I review my notes and practice problems. This is normal and effective study behavior. I don't have rituals or compulsive behaviors. My study routine is flexible and I adjust it based on what is actually needed without distress.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: If I miss a planned study session I simply reschedule it the next day without much distress. I am adaptable. Life sometimes interrupts plans and I accept this reality with equanimity. No strong attachment to rigid routines that I cannot deviate from.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: Functioning is consistently good. I attend college, play cricket with friends on weekends, maintain my social life. Academic engagement is healthy and purposeful. It does not significantly impair any area of my daily life or relationships.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: I feel well in control of my thoughts and behaviors. I can choose to study or take a break. My thoughts are manageable and proportionate. I handle stress by talking to friends or family. I feel genuinely capable of managing my life's demands.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: Normal life thoughts come and go — about work projects, family plans, financial goals. I also get creative ideas for my business at random times which I note down. These are welcome thoughts, not unwanted intrusions causing distress.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: I have good daily habits — morning exercise, checking business accounts once in evening, planning next day before sleep. These are healthy organizational routines I genuinely enjoy, not compulsive acts driven by fear or anxiety. I feel worse if I skip exercise, better if I do it.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: If business demands or family needs disrupt my routine I adapt without significant distress. I understand that life is unpredictable. I might feel slightly off on such days but it resolves quickly. No rituals that I feel compelled to complete regardless of circumstances.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: My daily functioning is smooth and satisfying. Business is running well, family life is good, health is maintained. My habits and routines support my functioning positively rather than consuming time or causing distress or problems.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Very good control over both my thoughts and actions. I make conscious choices about how I spend my time and mental energy. I don't feel driven by unwanted thoughts or compulsive impulses. I feel psychologically stable and well-grounded in my daily life.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: I sometimes think about my patients after particularly challenging cases, wondering if I could have done something differently. This is normal professional reflection, not intrusive and distressing obsession. It usually leads to learning and improvement in my practice.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: I double-check prescriptions before signing them as standard medical practice. I wash hands according to clinical hygiene protocols. These are professional requirements and good habits, not anxiety-driven compulsive rituals that I cannot control or stop.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: Emergency situations sometimes require deviating from standard workflows. I adapt to clinical needs without significant personal distress. Professional protocols exist for patient safety, not for managing my personal anxiety. I can be flexible when the situation genuinely requires it.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: My professional functioning is strong and I find my work deeply meaningful and rewarding. Personal life is balanced. I have good boundaries between professional concerns and personal time. My habits support excellent functioning rather than impairing it.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: I feel good control over my thoughts and clinical behaviors. I engage in purposeful reflection rather than uncontrollable rumination. My professional practices are conscious and evidence-based. I feel psychologically healthy and genuinely in command of my mental processes.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: Umm... I keep getting the thought that I may have made an error in my accounts work even after checking multiple times. The thought just doesn't leave. It's very disturbing.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: Hmm I recheck the same entries four five times. I know I have checked but still my mind says check again. I stay late in office only for this.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: Umm if I can't recheck I feel very uncomfortable the whole day. Like something bad is definitely going to happen because I missed verifying.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: Hmm my work hours increased a lot because of all the rechecking. Colleagues go home on time but I stay. My family is unhappy about this.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Umm very little. I try to tell myself it is fine but the doubt comes back immediately. It is like my brain doesn't trust my own checking.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: Hmm I get thoughts that my hands are dirty and contaminated even after washing. Sometimes when touching food I think I may be spreading germs to my family.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: Umm I wash hands minimum 15 times a day. Before and after every single activity. My hands are very dry and cracked now.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: Hmm if water is not available or I'm somewhere I can't wash properly I feel very distressed. I avoid touching anything until I can wash.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: Umm cooking takes very long because I keep washing hands in between. Going out is difficult because of contamination fear. My daily routine takes double time.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Hmm almost no control. I know it is excessive but the anxiety when I don't wash is too much to handle.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: Umm, I get thoughts that I may have said something offensive to someone in a conversation even if the conversation was totally normal. I keep replaying it in my head.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: Hmm I text the person again and again to apologise or check if they are okay. They must find it strange. I also replay the conversation mentally for hours.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: Umm if I can't send a follow-up message or replay the conversation I feel extreme guilt. Like I definitely must have said something wrong.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: Hmm I spend hours mentally reviewing conversations. It affects my sleep, my focus. I avoid talking to new people because the reviewing afterwards is exhausting.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Umm very little. I have tried to stop but the guilt feeling becomes unbearable and I give in to the reviewing again.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: Hmm I get thoughts that something bad will happen to my parents if I don't perform my morning puja correctly. The thought is very strong and scary.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: Umm I do puja for almost one hour every morning. If any step feels wrong I restart from beginning. Sometimes I am late to office because of this.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: Hmm if I miss puja or do it incorrectly I feel very anxious the whole day. I keep thinking something bad will happen and it will be my fault.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: Umm the morning puja takes so much time now. I am always late. My colleagues notice. At home also my parents say why so much time in puja.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Hmm I have tried to reduce the time but the fear is very strong. I cannot stop myself from repeating steps.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: Umm I get unwanted violent images sometimes, like I might push someone in front of a train or harm myself, even though I never want to do these things. It terrifies me.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: Hmm when these thoughts come I immediately move away from the person or place. I seek reassurance from my wife that I am a good person. I also avoid crowded platforms.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: Umm if I can't get reassurance my anxiety goes very high. I feel like maybe the thought means I am actually dangerous which scares me more.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: Hmm I stopped using certain metro routes. I ask for reassurance so much that my wife is frustrated. I can't explain to anyone because it sounds scary even though I would never act on it.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Umm very little control over thoughts coming. But I want to clarify I have full control over my actions, I just cannot control the thought arriving.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: Hmm normal daily thoughts, like pending tasks or what to cook. Nothing that I cannot dismiss when I want to.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: Umm I check door lock once before sleeping. Normal precaution. No repetitive behaviors beyond reasonable habits.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: Hmm if I miss my morning walk I feel slightly off but I adapt. It doesn't cause significant distress.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: Umm no real impact. My routine runs smoothly. I complete work on time and have a normal personal life.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Hmm good control. I can dismiss unwanted thoughts easily. I don't feel compelled to do anything repetitively.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: Umm thoughts about tomorrow's class preparation come to mind in evenings. Normal teacher habit, not intrusive or distressing.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: Hmm I prepare my lesson plan the night before. A good routine not a compulsive one. I do it once and feel satisfied.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: Umm if I skip preparation due to some reason I feel mildly unprepared but I manage the class anyway without major anxiety.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: Hmm no negative impact. My teaching is good and personal life is balanced. Habits support my work positively.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Umm full control. My habits are conscious choices. Nothing feels compulsive or uncontrollable in my daily routine.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: Hmm ideas for new business strategies come to mind often. I welcome these. No unwanted or distressing intrusive thoughts.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: Umm I have systematic habits like reviewing accounts weekly. These are practical business habits, not anxiety-driven rituals.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: Hmm if weekly review is delayed I reschedule without distress. Business needs flexibility and I have that.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: Umm positively. My structured habits make the business run well. No compulsive behaviors interfering with daily life.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Hmm complete control. I decide what to focus on. My mind feels organized and not driven by unwanted impulses.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: Umm sometimes I think about an upcoming interview or trip. Practical anticipatory thoughts, not intrusive ones that disturb me.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: Hmm I make a checklist before travel. Normal preparation. I do it once and feel ready without needing to recheck excessively.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: Umm I adapt easily. Flexibility is important and I manage well without sticking rigidly to any particular routine.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: Hmm good functioning overall. Studies, personal life and hobbies are all balanced. No compulsive interference.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Umm strong control. I choose my behaviors consciously. No sense of being driven by uncontrollable urges or fears.

responses.question: Describe thoughts that repeatedly enter your mind even when you try to ignore them.
responses.answer: Hmm after a patient visit I sometimes recall if I gave right advice. Normal professional reflection. It doesn't become obsessive.

responses.question: Explain any repetitive behaviors you perform to reduce discomfort or anxiety.
responses.answer: Umm I document my consultations carefully as standard practice. Not due to anxiety but professional habit.

responses.question: How do you feel if you are unable to complete a routine or ritual?
responses.answer: Hmm documentation delays happen sometimes. I catch up later without excessive worry. Adaptable.

responses.question: Describe how these thoughts or behaviors affect your daily functioning.
responses.answer: Umm my practice runs well. Home life is balanced. No compulsive behaviors creating problems in either area.

responses.question: How much control do you feel you have over these thoughts or actions?
responses.answer: Hmm good control. Professional diligence and OCD are different things and I don't experience the latter.

responses.question: Describe how you usually manage tasks that require planning and multiple steps. What challenges do you face?
responses.answer: Multi-step tasks are my biggest struggle. I start one thing, then notice something else, and completely forget about the first task. I started filing my ITR last month, went to find a document, got distracted by a WhatsApp message, and by evening I had forgotten about ITR completely. My wife ended up filing it for me at the last minute.

responses.question: When you have deadlines, how do you typically start and complete your work?
responses.answer: I always wait until the very last minute and then rush in a panic. I genuinely intend to start early every time but the deadline doesn't feel real until it is very close. Then I work with intense focus under pressure and often submit incomplete or error-prone work. My manager calls it a pattern and she is absolutely right.

responses.question: Explain how distractions (noise, people, notifications) affect your daily activities.
responses.answer: Distractions completely derail me. In open office environment I cannot work at all — every conversation I overhear, every movement pulls my attention away completely. My phone notifications especially destroy my focus. I have tried putting it on silent but then I keep manually checking it every two minutes.

responses.question: Describe situations where you feel physically restless or mentally overactive.
responses.answer: In long meetings I physically cannot sit still. I click my pen, tap my feet, shift in my chair constantly. My leg shakes involuntarily. At home I find it very hard to sit and watch a complete movie. I am always getting up to do something else every 10 minutes.

responses.question: How do attention or organization difficulties impact your studies, work, or relationships?
responses.answer: I was always told I am lazy or careless but I genuinely try very hard. I missed my sister's birthday because I forgot to note it down. I have been passed over for promotion because of my disorganization and inconsistency despite being told I am talented and creative. My wife handles almost all important paperwork.

responses.question: Describe how you usually manage tasks that require planning and multiple steps. What challenges do you face?
responses.answer: I am in Class 10 and studying for boards is very difficult for me. I make a timetable every week but I never follow it beyond the first day. When I sit to study I remember I need water, then I check my phone, then I start thinking about something completely unrelated. Hours pass without any real studying happening.

responses.question: When you have deadlines, how do you typically start and complete your work?
responses.answer: I always leave assignments for the night before they are due. Sometimes I submit incomplete assignments and lose marks. I have been given detention multiple times for late work. I feel terrible about it but I cannot explain to my teacher why I cannot start earlier even though I keep trying.

responses.question: Explain how distractions (noise, people, notifications) affect your daily activities.
responses.answer: In class if someone is talking somewhere in the room or if there is any sound outside I cannot focus on the teacher at all. My mind just goes there. I have been told to pay attention by multiple teachers but it is genuinely not in my control. I miss instructions and then have to ask my friends what was said.

responses.question: Describe situations where you feel physically restless or mentally overactive.
responses.answer: I cannot sit in one place during class at all. I doodle constantly during lectures. During family functions or religious ceremonies where I must sit quietly I feel unbearable restlessness and get scolded repeatedly for fidgeting. My parents think I am doing it deliberately to irritate them.

responses.question: How do attention or organization difficulties impact your studies, work, or relationships?
responses.answer: My marks are much lower than they should be considering I am actually quite intelligent. My science teacher says I understand concepts quickly but lose marks in exams because of careless mistakes and incomplete answers. Friends get frustrated when I forget plans we made. Parents have been called to school twice this year.

responses.question: Describe how you usually manage tasks that require planning and multiple steps. What challenges do you face?
responses.answer: I am a homemaker and managing household finances and planning is very hard for me. I started organizing the kitchen last week, remembered I needed to pay the electricity bill, went to get my phone to pay it, saw a notification, and two hours later the kitchen was still half-organized and bill was still not paid.

responses.question: When you have deadlines, how do you typically start and complete your work?
responses.answer: I have missed the school fee deadline three times this year. I remember it needs to be paid but keep thinking I will do it later. Then suddenly it is overdue. My husband takes out time from his work to remind me because he says I cannot be trusted with these things, which makes me feel very bad about myself.

responses.question: Explain how distractions (noise, people, notifications) affect your daily activities.
responses.answer: I start cooking, TV in background has something interesting, I go to watch for a minute, next thing food is burnt. I have burnt food many times this year. I keep multiple browser tabs open and jump between them. My mother-in-law complains I am always distracted and never finish what I start.

responses.question: Describe situations where you feel physically restless or mentally overactive.
responses.answer: At night my mind races with all the things I forgot to do during the day. I cannot fall asleep for hours. During family religious events where everyone must sit for long periods I feel extremely restless and uncomfortable. My mind jumps from thought to thought constantly.

responses.question: How do attention or organization difficulties impact your studies, work, or relationships?
responses.answer: My husband is a patient man but he is frustrated by my disorganization. I feel very guilty that I cannot manage what seems like simple household responsibilities. Before marriage I was always called careless and scattered. I feel like something is fundamentally wrong with me but nobody has ever given a name to it.

responses.question: Describe how you usually manage tasks that require planning and multiple steps. What challenges do you face?
responses.answer: I am a software developer and while I can code very well, project management is a disaster for me. I am excellent at the interesting coding parts but documentation, testing, and administrative tasks pile up endlessly. I lose track of sub-tasks constantly. My team lead has given me a performance improvement plan specifically for organizational skills.

responses.question: When you have deadlines, how do you typically start and complete your work?
responses.answer: Interesting technical problems I start immediately and work on for hours without break, sometimes forgetting meals entirely. But boring tasks like writing documentation or filling timesheets I procrastinate until the last possible moment, often missing them entirely. My performance is completely inconsistent and unpredictable.

responses.question: Explain how distractions (noise, people, notifications) affect your daily activities.
responses.answer: I cannot code in the office at all. The conversations, movement around me are completely distracting. I come into office at 9 PM to work productively in silence. My team thinks I am antisocial or arrogant but actually I just cannot function in noise. GitHub notifications interrupt my deep focus completely.

responses.question: Describe situations where you feel physically restless or mentally overactive.
responses.answer: When I am in hyperfocus on an interesting problem nothing can stop me, I work 14 hours straight. But in boring meetings my mind wanders so completely that I embarrassingly have no idea what was discussed when someone asks my opinion. I have started taking notes just to fake attentiveness.

responses.question: How do attention or organization difficulties impact your studies, work, or relationships?
responses.answer: Despite being technically strong I have been rejected from senior developer positions because interviewers perceive me as disorganized and scattered. My girlfriend left last year saying I never remember important dates or follow through on plans.
